_note: ORIGINAL content. Think C++ and the Google C++ Class outline informed coverage only; no prose, examples, or exercises were copied.
title: Syntax
summary: See how a small C++ program is put together and run.
refresher: A small C++ program usually includes a header, defines `main`, and places statements between braces. Most statements end with a semicolon. `std::cout` prints information on the screen.

sections.title: The shape of a C++ program
sections.summary: Start with the parts C++ expects: a header, `main`, braces, and statements.

blocks.body: C++ needs more setup than Python. A small program usually includes a header, starts in `main`, and puts the work between `{` and `}`. Most action lines end with a semicolon.

blocks.title: If you have seen Python
blocks.body: Python can run `print("Hi")` by itself. In C++, the print line goes inside `main`, and the program is compiled before it runs.

blocks.caption: Read this from top to bottom: include the print tool, start `main`, print one line, and finish.
The semicolon finishes the output statement. The braces hold the body of `main`.

sections.title: Printing text, numbers, and stored values
sections.summary: `std::cout` sends values to the screen. It can print text, math results, and variables.

blocks.body: `std::cout` prints what you send into it with `<<`. Text goes in quotes. Numbers can be used directly. A variable prints the value it holds.

blocks.caption: The first output prints math. The second output prints text and a variable.

blocks.body: C++ does the math before printing. If both sides of `/` are integers, the answer is an integer too. That is why `7 / 2` becomes `3` instead of `3.5`.

blocks.title: New lines
blocks.body: The characters `\n` mean move to the next line. They are written inside a string, like `"A\nB"`.

sections.title: Small symbols that change the program
sections.summary: Comments, capital letters, and parentheses all matter in C++.

blocks.body: A comment is a note for people reading the code. C++ ignores anything after `//` on that line.
C++ is case-sensitive. That means `cout`, `Cout`, and `COUT` are three different names. The print stream is `std::cout` with a lowercase `c`.

blocks.caption: An `if` condition needs parentheses. The statements that belong to the `if` go inside braces.

blocks.title: Start with the first compiler error
blocks.body: One missing semicolon, quote, parenthesis, or brace can cause several compiler messages. Fix the earliest message first because the later ones may disappear.

sections.title: Check your understanding
sections.summary: Try these before moving into the Syntax practice questions.

blocks.prompt: Which line is a complete output statement?
blocks.why: Output uses `<<`, the text is in quotes, and the statement ends with a semicolon.

blocks.prompt: Why does this fail to compile?
choices: The word score must be uppercase.
The output statement cannot be inside an if.
The braces should be square brackets.
blocks.why: C++ writes the condition as `if (score >= 70)`. The braces then hold the code that runs when the condition is true.

blocks.title: Syntax checklist
items: Use the exact capital letters in each name.
